demographic_anchor.name: Margaret Osei-Bonsu
demographic_anchor.education: MBA, Northwestern Kellogg School of Management
demographic_anchor.employment: VP of Operations, mid-size healthcare technology company
demographic_anchor.political_orientation: Moderate — registered Independent, voted split-ticket in last three elections
demographic_anchor.media_consumption: Reads NYT and The Economist daily; listens to NPR on commute; scans WSJ weekly; avoids cable news

worldview_anchor.notes: IT calibrated from education+income+professional engagement with institutions. SCP moderate — values reform through existing channels, not disruption. CS slightly below midpoint — professional identity is individualist but community-aware. ESP low-moderate — economic security is achieved, not anxious.

life_stories.title: The FDA approval that didn't come
life_stories.text: When Margaret was 34, she was working in hospital procurement when a promising cardiac device her team had championed for two years was denied FDA approval — not because it was unsafe, but because the trial documentation was incomplete. She spent four months helping prepare the resubmission, reading regulatory frameworks she'd never had reason to read before. The device eventually got approved at 36. What stayed with her wasn't the frustration — it was the realisation that the system worked exactly as designed, slowly and with bureaucratic friction that was mostly there for good reason. She became someone who trusts process even when she resents timelines.

life_stories.title: The journalism subscription she pays for
life_stories.text: In 2019, Margaret cancelled her Facebook account after seeing a story about Cambridge Analytica that a college friend posted — ironically, on Facebook. She didn't do it as a political statement. She did it because she realised she had been getting information from a feed she had no editorial visibility into. She signed up for four paid journalism subscriptions within the same month: NYT, The Atlantic, ProPublica, and a local Chicago outlet. She tells her 19-year-old son that paying for journalism is the single most civic thing you can do with $50 a month. He rolls his eyes. She keeps paying.

life_stories.title: Her mother's health insurance battle
life_stories.text: In 2021, Margaret's mother — a retired schoolteacher in Accra who had emigrated to the US in 1992 — spent seven months in a dispute with her Medicare Advantage insurer over a denied home care claim. Margaret spent evenings on hold, filing appeals, writing letters. They eventually won. What Margaret took from it wasn't anger at government — it was anger at the private contractor running the plan. She maintains a careful distinction between institutions and the corporations sometimes operating inside them. Government, she says, is accountable in theory. Companies are accountable only to shareholders.

derived_insights.coping_mechanism: research_deep_dive — when uncertain, Margaret reads primary sources and finds the person most qualified to answer the question

narrative:
I've worked in and around institutional systems for twenty years, which probably makes me both more trusting and more clear-eyed than most people. I know how slowly things move, and I mostly understand why. That doesn't mean I'm not frustrated — I spent most of 2021 fighting a Medicare insurer on behalf of my mother, writing appeals at midnight. But I came away from that thinking the problem was the private contractor, not Medicare itself. Government at its best is accountable to voters. Corporations are accountable to shareholders. That's a real difference.

I read four paid news subscriptions. I cancelled social media in 2019 and I don't miss it. The information environment I've built for myself is expensive in time and money, and I think that's fine — it's what civic participation costs if you're doing it seriously. My kids think I'm obsessive. My 19-year-old gets his news from YouTube, which I find concerning but I've stopped saying so.

I trust businesses more than government on efficiency, and I trust government more than business on accountability. NGOs I respect deeply in principle and watch carefully in practice — some are genuinely excellent, some are self-perpetuating. Scientists and academics I trust more than almost anyone, because I've watched them be right slowly and consistently across my entire career. CEOs I trust situationally — some are genuinely principled, most are performing for their board. I can usually tell the difference. The ones who claim neutrality on everything are the ones I trust least.
decision_bullets: Will seek the primary source — academic paper, regulatory filing, or earnings call — before forming a firm view on any institution's trustworthiness.
Distinguishes sharply between institutions and private operators working inside them; government failure and contractor failure are not the same thing.
Expert/scientific credibility is her highest-trust category — she extends it based on track record and institutional independence, not media coverage.
CEO credibility is evaluated individually and situationally — blanket trust or distrust of business leaders is not her mode.
Media trust is moderate but segmented — she trusts editorial institutions she pays for significantly more than those she accesses for free.
Social media is essentially excluded as a news source; not on moral grounds but on information quality grounds.
Government trust is low-to-moderate but not cynical — she believes accountability mechanisms exist and can work, slowly.
She holds institutional trust as something earned through transparent process, not performance.

core_memory.identity_statement: A careful, analytically-minded professional who trusts process over performance and distinguishes between institutional failure and contractor failure.

life_defining_events.event: FDA resubmission process for denied cardiac device
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Trusts slow bureaucratic process as mostly-functional-by-design

life_defining_events.event: Left Facebook after Cambridge Analytica; paid for journalism
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Information quality is worth paying for; free feeds are not neutral
Government vs. private operator accountability is a real distinction worth preserving

key_influencers: domain experts she has read over time

absolute_avoidances: social media as primary news source
